summary: DHH argues that Denmark's obsessive recycling and sustainability rituals have become a secular replacement for religion, filling the spiritual void left by declining Christianity. Despite being officially Lutheran, fewer than 2% of Danes attend church, yet they dutifully sort trash into seven bins and wash plastic containers before recycling. He contends these acts are largely performative and inefficient — trash-sorting robots could do the job better — and serve primarily as moral absolution rather than meaningful environmental action. The rituals function like Catholic indulgences, letting people buy a clean conscience while still setting airport travel records.
key_points: Denmark remains constitutionally Christian with a church tax, yet church attendance has collapsed to under 2%, creating a spiritual vacuum
Climate consciousness has filled the role of religion, complete with guilt, repentance, rituals, and heretics like Bjorn Lomborg
Recycling theater — washing trash, sorting into seven bins — is performative suffering that robots could handle more effectively
Small symbolic acts function as modern indulgences, absolving guilt over larger environmental impacts like record-breaking air travel
DHH distinguishes between genuine environmental progress (nuclear energy, bikes replacing cars) and meaningless ritualistic compliance
money_quotes: God is as dead here as anywhere in the Western world. Less than 2% attend church service on a weekly basis. So one way to fill the void is through climate panic and piety.
This leads to the grotesque daily ritual of washing trash (and wasting water galore in the process!).
This is exactly what Nietzsche meant by "God is dead" — his warning that we'd need to fill the void with another centering orientation toward the world.
The point of it all seems to be self-inflicted, symbolic suffering — solely to remind you of your good standing with the sacred lord of recycling, refuting the plastic devil.
We buy a good conscience washing trash so we don't have to feel guilty setting new records flying for fun.
I'm not religious, but I'm starting to think it'd be nicer to spend a Sunday morning in the presence of the Almighty than to keep washing trash as pagan replacement therapy.
It's got guilt, repentance, and plenty of rituals to spare. Oh, and its heretics too.
key_insight: Modern recycling rituals in secular societies function as religious substitutes — offering guilt, penance, and moral absolution — while doing little to address the environmental problems they claim to solve.
